content.text: LangChain Expression Language (LCEL) is the composition system that powers all LangChain pipelines. It defines one rule: any object that implements the
protocol can be joined to any other
operator. The result is itself a
, so chains compose indefinitely without special wiring.

attrs.title: LCEL: every component exposes the same Runnable interface

content.text: Every component — a prompt, an LLM, a parser, a retriever, a custom function — implements the same five-method interface:
that passes the output of each step as the input to the next.

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm    = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([("human", "Name three facts about {topic}.")])
parser = StrOutputParser()

# Pipe operator — identical to .pipe()
chain = prompt | llm | parser

# Equivalent explicit form
chain = prompt.pipe(llm).pipe(parser)

# Invoke
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "black holes"})
print(result)

# The full chain is itself a Runnable — it can be used inside another chain
meta_chain = prompt | llm | parser | (lambda text: text.upper())
# The lambda is auto-wrapped in a RunnableLambda
LCEL propagates streaming through every step in the chain automatically. When the LLM emits a token chunk, the parser and any downstream
components receive and forward it as soon as it arrives — no buffering to wait for the complete response.

# Sync streaming — prints tokens as they arrive
for chunk in chain.stream({"topic": "quantum computing"}):
    print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
print()

# Async streaming — for use in async servers
import asyncio

async def stream_to_client():
    async for chunk in chain.astream({"topic": "machine learning"}):
        # In a FastAPI endpoint: await websocket.send_text(chunk)
        print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
    print()

asyncio.run(stream_to_client())
emits structured events at each step of the chain — useful for building UIs that show per-step status.

async def show_events():
    async for event in chain.astream_events(
        {"topic": "LCEL"},
        version="v2",
    ):
        kind = event["event"]
        if kind == "on_chat_model_stream":
            chunk = event["data"]["chunk"].content
            print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
        elif kind == "on_chain_start":
            print(f"\n[starting {event['name']}]")
        elif kind == "on_chain_end":
            print(f"\n[done {event['name']}]")

asyncio.run(show_events())
runs multiple inputs concurrently using a thread pool. The
caps parallel API calls to avoid rate-limit errors.

topics = [
    {"topic": "neural networks"},
    {"topic": "gradient descent"},
    {"topic": "transformers"},
    {"topic": "reinforcement learning"},
    {"topic": "generative adversarial networks"},
]

# Run all five concurrently but cap at 3 simultaneous API calls
results = chain.batch(topics, config={"max_concurrency": 3})
for topic_input, text in zip(topics, results):
    print(f"{topic_input['topic']}: {text[:80]}...")

# Async batch — for use inside async code
async def async_batch():
    results = await chain.abatch(topics, config={"max_concurrency": 3})
    return results
Any Python function — sync or async — can be inserted into a chain with
, LCEL passes the lambda a generator of chunks; the lambda can itself be a generator to forward them.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def add_word_count(text: str) -> dict:
    return {"text": text, "word_count": len(text.split())}

# Auto-wrapped: lambda functions work the same way
chain_with_count = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser() | add_word_count

result = chain_with_count.invoke({"topic": "entropy"})
print(result["word_count"])   # e.g. 87

# Async lambda — used transparently by ainvoke/astream
async def async_clean(text: str) -> str:
    return text.strip().replace("  ", " ")

chain_async = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser() | RunnableLambda(async_clean)
clean = await chain_async.ainvoke({"topic": "entropy"})
runs multiple chains on the same input concurrently and merges their outputs into a single dict.
is a convenience method that adds keys to a dict input without replacing the existing ones.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel, RunnablePassthrough

# Two chains that both receive the same {"topic": ...} input
summary_chain  = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()
keywords_chain = (
    ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([("human", "List 5 keywords for {topic}, comma-separated.")])
    | llm | StrOutputParser()
)

parallel = RunnableParallel(
    summary=summary_chain,
    keywords=keywords_chain,
)
result = parallel.invoke({"topic": "black holes"})
print(result["summary"][:80])
print(result["keywords"])

# .assign() — adds keys to the pipeline dict without dropping existing ones
retrieval_chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        context=lambda x: retrieve_docs(x["question"])   # adds "context" key
    )
    | answer_prompt
    | llm
    | StrOutputParser()
)
Components can expose parameters that callers override at runtime via
. This lets you build one chain and vary temperature, model, or system prompt per request without rebuilding the chain.

from langchain_core.runnables import ConfigurableField

llm_configurable = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    temperature=0.2,
).configurable_fields(
    temperature=ConfigurableField(
        id="llm_temperature",
        name="LLM Temperature",
        description="Controls output randomness; 0 = deterministic, 1 = creative",
    )
)

chain = prompt | llm_configurable | StrOutputParser()

# Default (temperature=0.2)
result_default = chain.invoke({"topic": "poetry"})

# Override temperature at runtime
result_creative = chain.with_config(
    configurable={"llm_temperature": 0.9}
).invoke({"topic": "poetry"})

# Override a model alternative
llm_alternatives = llm_configurable.configurable_alternatives(
    ConfigurableField(id="llm_variant"),
    default_key="haiku",
    sonnet=ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5"),
)
chain_alt = prompt | llm_alternatives | StrOutputParser()
result_sonnet = chain_alt.with_config(
    configurable={"llm_variant": "sonnet"}
).invoke({"topic": "poetry"})
to attach fixed arguments to a runnable — the bound values are merged with the runtime input on every call. This is how you attach tools or stop sequences to an LLM.

from langchain_core.tools import tool

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Return current weather for a city."""
    return f"Sunny, 22°C in {city}"

@tool
def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Search the web for a query."""
    return f"Top result for '{query}': ..."

# Bind tools to the LLM — they become available on every call
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools([get_weather, web_search])

# Bind a stop sequence — LLM stops generating when it emits "DONE"
llm_with_stop = llm.bind(stop=["DONE"])

# Bind default invoke kwargs — useful for passing metadata to callbacks
llm_tagged = llm.bind(
    tags=["production"],
    metadata={"pipeline": "customer-support"},
)
LCEL chains carry type information and can be introspected without running them. This is useful for debugging, documentation, and optimisation.

chain = prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()

# Print the chain's schema — shows input/output types
print(chain.input_schema.schema())
# {'properties': {'topic': {'title': 'Topic', 'type': 'string'}}, ...}

print(chain.output_schema.schema())
# {'title': 'StrOutputParserOutput', 'type': 'string'}

# Get a Graphviz-compatible graph representation
graph = chain.get_graph()
graph.print_ascii()
# +----------------------------------+
# | ChatPromptTemplate               |
# +----------------------------------+
#              *
#              *
# +----------------------------------+
# | ChatAnthropic                    |
# +----------------------------------+
#              *
#              *
# +----------------------------------+
# | StrOutputParser                  |
# +----------------------------------+
subclasses with fixed method signatures. LCEL replaces all of that with composition:
Real-World Pattern: Streaming RAG with Fallback

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

# Primary and fallback LLMs
primary = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5", temperature=0)
fallback = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", temperature=0)

rag_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only the context below.\n\nContext:\n{context}"),
    ("human", "{question}"),
])

def format_docs(docs):
    return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

# Full pipeline: retrieve → format → answer → stream
rag_chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        context=lambda x: format_docs(retriever.invoke(x["question"]))
    )
    | rag_prompt
    | primary.with_fallbacks([fallback])   # automatic failover
    | StrOutputParser()
)

# Stream the answer token-by-token
async def answer_streaming(question: str):
    async for chunk in rag_chain.astream({"question": question}):
        yield chunk   # forward to HTTP client as SSE

# Batch multiple questions at once
answers = rag_chain.batch(
    [{"question": q} for q in ["What is X?", "How does Y work?"]],
    config={"max_concurrency": 2},
)
LCEL provides two mechanisms for handling failures:
retries the same runnable on transient errors, while
switches to an alternative runnable when the primary fails.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableRetry
import anthropic

# Retry on rate-limit errors with exponential backoff
llm_with_retry = llm.with_retry(
    retry_if_exception_type=(anthropic.RateLimitError, anthropic.APITimeoutError),
    wait_exponential_jitter=True,   # adds random jitter to avoid thundering herd
    stop_after_attempt=4,
)

# Fallback chain: try Sonnet first, fall back to Haiku
sonnet = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5")
haiku  = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

robust_llm = sonnet.with_fallbacks(
    [haiku],
    exceptions_to_handle=(anthropic.APIStatusError,),
)

# Combine retry + fallback
production_llm = (
    sonnet
    .with_retry(stop_after_attempt=2)
    .with_fallbacks([haiku.with_retry(stop_after_attempt=3)])
)

chain = prompt | production_llm | StrOutputParser()
dict. Tags, metadata, and callbacks in the config flow through the entire chain automatically — you do not need to thread them through each step manually.
Streaming propagates automatically through the chain without buffering, enabling low-latency UIs.
add parallel branches and dict-key extension without extra wiring.
let a single chain serve multiple configurations without rebuilding.
to introspect a chain without running it.
